coredata.dc:title: Cationic fluorescent carbon dots with solution ultra-stability and its rapid/on-site sensing application for HClO
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Linlin
Li, Lingfei
Liu, Renyong
Xie, Chenggen
Zhao, Jun
Chang, Wengui
Chen, Lijuan
Yan, Yehan
Zhang, Ningning
Zhang, Wei
Liu, Bianhua
Yang, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) as a remarkable fluorescent nanomaterial have the advantages of easy preparation, good photostability and high sensitivity. However, the poor aqueous solution stability of carbon dots largely limited their practical application due to the characteristic of easily forming precipitation for long time storage. Here, a kind of cationic fluorescent carbon dots CDs-P(Ph)3 was designed by introducing a cationic compound, (4-carboxybutyl) triphenyl phosphonium bromide, to construct an electrostatic shell outside the dots. Such electrostatic shell could highly improve carbon dots stability in an aqueous solution to make CDs-P(Ph)3 stable for long-term storage with negligible aggregation. Meanwhile, the sensitivity of CDs-P(Ph)3 for hypochlorous acid (HClO) was also enhanced on the basis of the electron-withdrawing effect of cationic substituents on the surface of carbon dots. The limit of detection of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO was as low as ∼0.32 μM. Additionally, the fluorescence of CDs-P(Ph)3 could be rapid quenched by HClO with a quenching efficiency of more than 80% within 30 s. The excellent stability of CDs-P(Ph)3 in an aqueous solution made it suitable for on-site detecting HClO in real samples, such as tap, well and lake water. Such designed fluorescent nanomaterial would provide a practical application pathway for optical sensing detection in environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cationic shell
Stability
Electron-withdrawing
Hypochlorous acid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dynamics of CDs-P(Ph)3 response to HClO
Fluorescence response of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO
Preparation of CDs-P(Ph)3 and its identification for HClO
Excellent dispersibility of CDs-P(Ph)3 and its high sensitivity for HClO
Structural characterization of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs
Optimal excitation wavelength screening of CDs-P(Ph)3 to HClO
Response mechanism of CDs-P(Ph)3 with HClO
The sensitivity and selectivity of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22274158 , 21804100 , 21976183 ), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Provincial (2208085MB30), Anhui West University Scientific Research Foundation for High-Level Talents (WGKQ2021001, WGKQ202001002 and WGKQ201702002), Nature Science Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Anhui Province (KJ2020ZD57), the Postdoctoral Research Project of West Anhui University ( WXBSH2021003, WSBSH2020001 ) and Anhui Provincial outstanding Young Talents Support Program for Colleges and Universities (gxyqZD2022073).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The stability experiment of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs after four months in room temperature. The responsive sensitivity of (B) CDs-P(Ph)3 and (C) CDs towards HClO in PBS buffer solution. The excitation wavelength was 390 nm.

(A) The zeta potentials of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs; (B–F) XPS spectra of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs, of which (B) XPS survey of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs, the spectra of high-resolution (C) C 1s, (D) N 1s, (E) O 1s and (F) P 2p of CDs-P(Ph)3.
(A) The reactive mechanism of CDs-P(Ph)3 with HClO. (B) The transmission electron microscopy (TEM), (C) UV–vis spectra, and (D) FT-IR spectra of CDs-P(Ph)3 without and with HClO.

(A) Fluorescent spectra of CDs-P(Ph)3 with different concentrations of HClO (0–80 μM) in PBS buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4). The inset was their corresponding fluorescent photographs that were taken by a digital camera under a 365 nm UV lamp. (B) The linear relationship of log (I/I0) vs the concertation of HClO. (C) The selectivity and interference experiments of CDs-P(Ph)3 for different sepcies, of which the sample number 1–15 represented blank, 80 μM HClO, 100 μM (O2 −, H2O2, •OH, TBHP, 1O2, TBO•, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Mg2+ and Cr2+), respectively.
The preparation of cationic carbon dots, CDs-P(Ph)3, with a solution ultra-stability and its high sensitivity for HClO.
The bond orders of CN and CC in the structures of CDs and CDs-P(Ph)3 calculated by employing DFT calculations with the B3LYP functional and def2TZVP basis sets.
Recovery rates for HClO in tap, lake and well water by CDs-P(Ph)3.

head.ce:title: Cationic fluorescent carbon dots with solution ultra-stability and its rapid/on-site sensing application for HClO

School of Materials and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui, 230031, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, Anhui, 230031, China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Linlin Yang and Lingfei Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a remarkable fluorescent nanomaterial have the advantages of easy preparation, good photostability and high sensitivity. However, the poor aqueous solution stability of carbon dots largely limited their practical application due to the characteristic of easily forming precipitation for long time storage. Here, a kind of cationic fluorescent carbon dots CDs-P(Ph)3 was designed by introducing a cationic compound, (4-carboxybutyl) triphenyl phosphonium bromide, to construct an electrostatic shell outside the dots. Such electrostatic shell could highly improve carbon dots stability in an aqueous solution to make CDs-P(Ph)3 stable for long-term storage with negligible aggregation. Meanwhile, the sensitivity of CDs-P(Ph)3 for hypochlorous acid (HClO) was also enhanced on the basis of the electron-withdrawing effect of cationic substituents on the surface of carbon dots. The limit of detection of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO was as low as ∼0.32 μM. Additionally, the fluorescence of CDs-P(Ph)3 could be rapid quenched by HClO with a quenching efficiency of more than 80% within 30 s. The excellent stability of CDs-P(Ph)3 in an aqueous solution made it suitable for on-site detecting HClO in real samples, such as tap, well and lake water. Such designed fluorescent nanomaterial would provide a practical application pathway for optical sensing detection in environmental samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanomaterials such as the carbon dots (CDs), quantum dots, nanoclusters have wide application prospects in the fields of environment analysis, biomarker detection and biological imaging [1–5], since they possess the characteristics of high fluorescence quantum yield, long fluorescent lifetime, narrow emission spectrum. Amount of them, CDs as a new nanomaterial with plentiful fluorescent colors is capable of being prepared by one-step syntheses from a variety of raw materials, such as chemicals, crops and foodstuff [2,6,7]. Most of CDs are suit as the fluorescent sensors for the detection of analyte in water due to the advantages of excellent luminescence performance, good anti-photobleaching, excellent water solubility. However, the dispersibility of most CDs in an aqueous solution is not very good, because nanoscale CDs are easy to aggregate and even to form precipitation after long-term storage, which seriously limits their application in real samples. Hence, increasing the storage stability of CDs with high concentration is an urgent problem to be solved. If the particles carry a large amount of charge on their peripheries to form electrostatic shells, the repulsion between them will keep particles away from each other, avoiding to produce aggregation and precipitation. The storage stability of CDs can be well solved by introducing some electriferous groups on their surfaces, which will dramatically enhance the application values in environment detection.

Hypochlorous acid (HClO) with strong oxidation plays a vital role in daily life and industry, such as the disinfection and sterilization of foodstuff, waterworks, and public place [8–10]. In water, HClO and ClO− formed by the reaction of chlorine with water have the ability to destroy 99.9% waterborne pathogens through denaturing protein, breaking the double-stranded DNA or rendering nucleic acid useless [11,12]. So employing an appropriate amount of HClO can achieve the aim of perfect sterilization. For example, the dosage of HClO that is used for sterilized pretreatment of tap water is stipulated ranging from 10 μM to 10 mM [13]. Once its dosage is excessively used in the process of water or foodstuff treatment, the remnant will serious influence organism health resulting in some adverse reactions, including contact dermatitis, vomiting and asthma [14,15]. Even worse, HClO can generate chlorinated compounds through reacting with a series of organic substances in an aqueous media, which is ferociously oncogenic to human [16]. With the enhancement of human's awareness about environment and food safety, many methods have been developed to be applied in the detection of the remaining harmful substances in foods, water, soil and atmospheric, such as colorimetry, electrochemistry and fluorescence method [17–19]. Among them, fluorescence method has the significant advantages of high detection sensitivity, short response time and diversified forms, which rapidly propels various types of fluorescent materials emergence [20–22]. For example, a large amount of molecular fluorescent sensors was developed for detecting HClO with high selectivity and sensitivity [23,24], while the synthetic and purification processes of these organic molecules usually were complicated and cumbersome. Although some fluorescent quantum dots with long emission wavelength had been used for HClO detection [25], their applications were also limited due to the disadvantages of harsh preparation condition and poor water solubility. Profiting from the remarkable optical character and easy to synthesize, CDs had become one of the typically fluorescent nanomaterials in the analysis and sensing fields. Hence, it is a good choice to develop a kind of new CDs with solution ultra-stability and on-site sensing application for HClO.

Here, we project to prepare new cationic fluorescent CDs that both have good solution stability to avoid aggregation and high sensitivity and selectivity for HClO, achieving the on-site and real-time detection of HClO in actual samples. Given that functional groups such as unsaturated CC, oxime (CN), and hydrazide are well-performance recognition sites for HClO [26–28], compound m-phenylenediamine was employed as a carbon source to form green CDs with a large amount of the above-mentioned functional groups, froming a luminescent center responsive to HClO. A larger cationic compound 4-carboxybutyl triphenylphosphonium bromide was chosen as a cation source to be intruduced onto the surface of CDs, constructing an electrostatic shell outside the CDs. In addition, the cationic groups with strong electron-withdrawing effect would also largely weaken the unsaturated bonds (CC/CN/C–N) adjacent to them. Based on these, the cationic carbon dots CDs-P(Ph)3 emitting bright green fluorescence were successfully prepared. Different from common CDs, the solution of CDs-P(Ph)3 with a high concentration was still clear or had a negligible sediment after storage of 4 months. Meanwhile, CDs-P(Ph)3 could rapidly and selectively react with HClO in the presence of other interferents, and the limit of detection was as low as ∼0.32 μM. The successful application of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO in real samples such as tap, well and lake water further showed that it was a very potential nano-chemosensor in the aspect of on-site detection.

4-Carboxybutyl triphenylphosphonium bromide and m-phenylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin. Sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), tert-butyl hydroperoxide (TBHP), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and potassium dioxide (KO2) were provided by Sigma Aldrich. Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O) were obtained from Chemical Reagent Company, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemical regents were analytical reagent grade, and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) used in all experiments was produced by Millipore water purification system.

The morphologies of CDs-P(Ph)3 were examined under transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL-2010). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on a Thermo Scientific™ K-Alpha™ + spectrometer equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) operating at 100 W. The UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and FT-IR spectra were recorded with Shimadzu UV-2550 spectrometer, Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer and Thermo Fisher Nicolet iS10 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer, respectively.

The compounds m-phenylenediamine with and without 4-carboxybutyl triphenylphosphonium bromide were severally dissolved by the same solvent, and subsequently transferred into two different stainless reaction stills, and then CDs and CDs-P(Ph)3 were prepared by using the hydrothermal method. Specifically, 0.12 g 4-carboxybutyl triphenylphosphonium bromide and 0.3 g m-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 35 mL of ethanol-water mix (Vethnol:Vwater = 4:3), and then sonicated for 15 min to dissolve them completely. Next, the solution was transferred into teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclaves to heated for 12 h under 180 °C. After cooled to room temperature, the solvent was removed using a vacuum rotary evaporator.

After adding 5 mL of ultrapure water, the system was sonicated for 30 min to sufficiently dissolve the crude products CDs-P(Ph)3. Then, the CDs-P(Ph)3 solution was centrifuged at a rotate speed of 8000 r/min for 10 min. The collected supernatant was dialyzed using a dialysis bag (500 molecular weight) in a 1000 mL beaker containing 800 mL ultra-pure water for 12 h to remove small impurities. After changing water four times, the CDs-P(Ph)3 solution outside (500 mL ultrapure water for 24 h) and inside of the dialysis bag were collected, and the former was concentrated to several milliliter by a vacuum rotary evaporator. Finally, both the above CDs-P(Ph)3 were mixed and stored at 4 °C for further use.

The preparation and purification of CDs was similar with that of CDs-P(Ph)3. The distinctions were that the raw material of CDs did not contain 4-carboxybutyl triphenylphosphonium bromide and the solvent was ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamics of CDs-P(Ph)3 response to HClO
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solution of CDs-P(Ph)3 was diluted using 10 mM PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) to make its fluorescence intensity ∼500 a. u., acting as the detection solution for HClO. And all the following experiments used the same detection solution. After the concentration of 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 and 70 μM HClO were severally added into 2 mL of detection solution, the fluorescence intensities of probe were recorded at different time points. Finally, the optimal time of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO were obtained from their kinetic spectral diagrams.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of dynamics of CDs-P(Ph)3 in response to HClO, the reactive time was confirmed as 3 min. Then, the concentrations of HClO ranging from 0 to 80 μM were separately added into different groups of detection solution (2 mL), and incubated for 3 min to obtain their fluorescence spectra. And their corresponding fluorescence photographs were taken by a digital camera under a 365 nm hand-held ultraviolet lamp.

In addition, the selective experiment was performed by adding other reactive oxygen species and metal ions into the probe's solution. According to the reported reference [29], we prepared the reactive oxygen species used in this work and also measured their real concentration. For example, we used 5.2% NaClO solution as the donor of HClO, which concentration was determined by the absorption at λ = 292 nm (ε = 350 M−1 cm−1). Superoxide anion (O2 −) was obtained by dissolving KO2 in DMSO, and hydroxyl radical (•OH) was generated by the Fenton reaction of FeSO4 and H2O2. The concentration of H2O2 was determined by its absorption at λ = 240 nm (ε = 43.6 M−1 cm−1), and tert-butyl peroxyl radical (TBO•) was generated instantaneously by the reaction of Fe2+ with TBHP.

A series of tap, well and lake water samples containing different concentrations of HClO were prepared by “spiking” them with different volumes of HClO stock solution. These real samples were then added into 2 mL of CDs-P(Ph)3 nanoprobe solution. After the resulting solutions were mixed well and incubated for 3 min, their emission spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 390 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs-P(Ph)3 and its identification for HClO
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the small particle size, large specific surface area and high surface energy, nanoparticles are prone to self-aggregation and agglomeration. This will cause particle size becoming larger to make them lose the characteristics of nanoparticles, bringing great difficulties for the preservation of nanoparticle. If the surfaces of nanoparticles have a large amount of charge, the electrostatic repulsion between particles can be used to prevent them agglomeration, which is beneficial to make them stay at a uniform dispersion state. In addition, because eyes are more sensitive to green light, it is a good choice to prepare the carbon dots emitting green fluorescence as a chemosensor for analyte. Hence, m-phenylenediamine and 4-carboxybutyl triphenylphosphonium bromide were severally employed as the carbon and cationic source to prepare CDs-P(Ph)3 emitting green fluorescence through hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). The cationic group triphenylphosphonium on the periphery of CDs-P(Ph)3 not only helped to improve the dispersibility of carbon dots in an aqueous solution, but also could weaken the bonds of CC/CN of carbon core owing to its strong electron-withdrawing effect. In the presence of oxidizing substance HClO, CDs-P(Ph)3 were easily broken into small-sized carbon dots, and the free –NH2 could be also oxidized into –NO2. Once the large π-conjugated structure and electropositivity of CDs-P(Ph)3 were destroyed, the size of these particles would become smaller and aggregate together along with remarkable fluorescence quenching to achieve the identification of HClO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Excellent dispersibility of CDs-P(Ph)3 and its high sensitivity for HClO
ce:section.ce:para: Given that the good stability of nanomaterials dispersed in water is a prior condition for their application, the dispersion of CDs-P(Ph)3 and common CDs solution was compared after they were putted for four months in room temperature. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the newly prepared stock solutions of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs were dark tan under natural light. After four months, it was founded that a large amount of precipitation was emerged on the bottom of bottle loading with CDs, while just a small amount of precipitation was observed on the bottom of bottle loading with CDs-P(Ph)3. In order to visually reflect their particle sizes, a red laser was employed to shed light on the solution of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs from one side, respectively. As anticipated, CDs-P(Ph)3 showed a bright path in the vertical direction of its incident light, namely Dündar phenomenon, meaning that CDs-P(Ph)3 had a solution ultra-stability. However, the solution of CDs did not form the same optical path, just displayed within a very small distance. In other words, the Dündar phenomenon could not be observed in the solution of CDs. This is because that CDs with low charge was easily combined with each other to form larger particles, demonstrating that the dispersibility of CDs became poor after long-term storage.

In addition, the solutions of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs were centrifuged by a centrifuge at a rotational speed of 8000 r/min for 10 min to obtain their supernatants. From the left photographs of Fig. 1A, it was found that the supernatant of CDs-P(Ph)3 was still dark tan, while that of CDs had become light yellow, further confirming that the better stability of CDs-P(Ph)3. In conclusion, the stability of CDs-P(Ph)3 solution is significantly better than that of CDs solution, meaning that CDs-P(Ph)3 was more suitable for application in the fields of environmental analysis and biological imaging.

Meanwhile, the sensitivities of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs for HClO were also studied by comparing their fluorescence intensity changes. Firstly, both the stock solution of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs were diluted in a PBS buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4) to make their fluorescence intensity ∼ 900 a. u. Using a PBS buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4). Subsequently, 80 μM of HClO was severally added into the detection solutions of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs, followed by reacting for 3 min, and then their fluorescence spectra were obtained. As shown in Fig. 1B and C, it was easily founded that HClO caused a more obvious decrease of fluorescence intensity of CDs-P(Ph)3 in comparison with that of CDs, meaning that CDs-P(Ph)3 was more sensitive for HClO. This is because the bonds of CC/CN in the carbon core of CDs-P(Ph)3 were greatly weakened by triphenylphosphonium due to its strong electron-withdrawing effect, which was destroyed by HClO more easily. In order to confirm this view, we used the Gaussian 16 program [30] to calculate the bond orders of some same bonding atoms of CDs and CDs-P(Ph)3. We first constructed the same carbon core structures of CDs and CDs-P(Ph)3 (Fig. S1), and then calculated their bond orders by using DFT calculations with the B3LYP functional and def2TZVP basis sets. The corresponding data of some CC and CN bond orders of them were severally listed in Table 1 .

It could be easily seen that the bond orders of N1–C3, N2–C4, N2–C5, C3–C7 and C5–C6 of CDs were larger than that of CDs-P(Ph)3, of which the bond orders N2–C4 and C5–C6 occurred a significant weakening from 1.07 to 0.99 and 1.44 to 1.33. As for the same type bonds, the bond strength is proportional to the bond order. So, the above results showed that the strong electron-withdrawing group triphenylphosphonium on the surface of carbon core played a key role in obviously decreasing the bond strengths of N1–C3, N2–C4, N2–C5, C3–C7 and C5–C6, making them more easily destroyed by HClO. Additionally, both the emmission peak of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs occurred slight blue shift after the addition of HClO (Fig. 1C and D), because their carbon cores were broken to small size substences emmiting very weaken fluorescence and their emission peaks located at the region of short wavelength. In sumaury, the introduction of cationic groups on CDs not only highly increased their solution stability for long time storage by virtue of the electrostatic repulsion, but also improved their sensitivity towards HClO due to the electron-withdrawing effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since zeta potential is an important indicator to measure the stability of particles in a suspended liquid system, the zeta potentials of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs were firstly measured. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the zeta potential of CDs-P(Ph)3 was 49.3 mV, while that of CDs was just 4.35 mV. The former largely outweighed the latter, showing that the introduction of triphenylphosphonium groups into the structure of carbon dots highly enhanced their positive charges. The higher charge density on the surface of CDs-P(Ph)3 made it more stable in the solution.

Meanwhile, both the structures of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to verify if triphenylphosphonium groups were successfully introduced onto the surface of CDs-P(Ph)3. Fig. 2B showed the XPS surveys of CDs-P(Ph)3 and CDs, it could be easily founded three typical peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV and 531 eV, which severally corresponding to the C 1s peak of sp2 carbon core, N 1s and O 1s peak [31]. However, the intensity of N 1s peak of CDs-P(Ph)3 was obviously weaker than that of CDs, demonstrating that most of –NH2 were occupied by the cationic groups through an acylation reaction of –NH2 with –COOH. Meanwhile, in the XPS surveys of CDs-P(Ph)3, a new peak emerged at 131.5 eV attributing to P 2p peak, verifying the successful modification of triphenylphosphonium groups onto the structure of CDs-P(Ph)3. Fig. 2C–F showed the spectra of high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, O 1s and P 2p of CDs-P(Ph)3, respectively. Fig. 2C displayed four distinct carbon peaks at 284.3 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.8 eV and 286.8 eV, which severely assigned to the bonds of CC/C–C, C–O–C/C–O–H, CN and CO [32]. The spectra of high-resolution N 1s exhibited two peaks at 398.3 eV, 399.4 eV attributing to CN, pyridinic N (Fig. 2D), and the O 1s spectra described the peaks of 531.8 eV, 533.4 eV for C–O–C, C–OH bonds (Fig. 2E) [33]. Fig. 2F showed the peaks at 132.6 eV and 133.5 eV in the spectrum of P 2p revealing the existence of CC–P and C–P bonds [34,35]. Additionally, the high-resolution spectra of common CDs were also analyzed in Fig. S2. It could be obviously founded that the corresponding peaks of C 1s, N 1s and O 1s were similar with that of CDs-P(Ph)3, demonstrating their same core of fluorescence emission. The above results verified that the triphenylphosphonium group was successfully decorated onto the structure of CDs-P(Ph)3, and played a key role in enhancing the properties of more superior dispersibility in water and better sensitivity for HClO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimal excitation wavelength screening of CDs-P(Ph)3 to HClO
ce:section.ce:para: Given that excitation wavelength has great effects on the fluorescence intensity of nanomaterials, the optimal excitation wavelength of CDs-P(Ph)3 should be selected first for analyte detection. To explore the influence of excitation wavelength on fluorescence intensity of CDs-P(Ph)3, the fluorescent spectra were obtained by using a serious of excitation wavelengths ranging from 350 to 420 nm (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. S3A, it could be easily founded that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-P(Ph)3 gradually enhanced along with the excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 390 nm, of which its fluorescent intensity increased ∼ 2.2 times. However, with the excitation wavelength continue to increase (390–420 nm), the fluorescence intensity of CDs-P(Ph)3 declined to about 70% of the one at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm rather than continue to increase (Fig. S3B). The above results demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-P(Ph)3 was the strongest when excited at 390 nm, indicating that 390 nm is the optimal excitation wavelength for detecting the target substance. Hence, all the following experiments chose 390 nm as the excitation wavelength of CDs-P(Ph)3.

The reactive dynamics of probe with analyte is another important factor to evaluate its practicability in real detection. Hence, dynamics experiments of CDs-P(Ph)3 with HClO in different concentrations were measured to explore their optimum response time. The spectra of fluorescence intensity vs time were obtained by collecting intensity values at different time points after the addition of 0–70 μM HClO, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence of CDs-P(Ph)3 was quickly decreased and its intensity reached a stable value within 30 s at the low HClO concentration of 0–30 μM. Similarly, at the high HClO concentration of 40–70 μM, the fluorescence of CDs-P(Ph)3 was also rapidly and dramatically quenched by HClO at the initial 30 s, but weakened slowly in the following time. It could be easily founded that the time it took to reach equilibrium depended on the concentration of HClO. The higher concentration of HClO was added into the CDs-P(Ph)3 solution, the longer time was consumed to reach stability. It could be easily founded that 70 μM HClO was able to almost completely quench CDs-P(Ph)3 fluorescence, and the time it took for the fluorescence to stabilize was 3 min. This time was long enough to ensure CDs-P(Ph)3 react with all the above concentrations of HClO completely, so it could serve as the equilibrium time of CDs-P(Ph)3 reacting with HClO in following experments.

In addition, the photostability of CDs-P(Ph)3 was studied under natural light for 7 d and a 365 nm UV light for 2 h. As shown in Fig. S5, it could be easily founded that the fluorescence intensity of CDs-P(Ph)3 almost remained a fixed value, demonstrating its excellent photostability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response mechanism of CDs-P(Ph)3 with HClO
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the response mechanism of CDs-P(Ph)3 with HClO, several characterization methods including TEM, XPS, UV–vis absorption and FT-IR spectrum were employed to investigate functional group changes on the surface of carbon dots. Given that the chemical bonds and functional groups, such as CC, CN, CO–NH and –NH2, were commonly oxidized into the bonds of C–Cl, C–OH, –COOH and –NO2 by HClO [36], the electrostatic shell of CDs-P(Ph)3 could be destroyed through the cleavage of CO–NH bonds that linked to the cationic groups, largely decreasing the stability of CDs-P(Ph)3 in an aqueous solution for long-term storage. Meanwhile, the carbon core was broken into small fragment through the destruction of CC and CN bonds, and electron donor group –NH2 was possibly oxidized into a strong electron-withdrawing group –NO2, causing significant fluorescence quenching and a slight emission peak blue shift of CDs-P(Ph)3 to realize HClO detection. Based on the above-mentioned reasons, the schematic diagram of CDs-P(Ph)3 with HClO was descripted in Fig. 3 A.

As shown in Fig. 3B, CDs-P(Ph)3 had a very good dispersibility in water due to the electrostatic repulsion of cationic groups on its surface. In the presence of HClO, CDs-P(Ph)3 became small and aggregated together, as shown in the area marked by white ring. This is because the structures of CDs-P(Ph)3 were broken by HClO through the cleavage of CC, CN or CO–NH bonds. The destroy of electrostatic shell formed by cationic group triphenylphosphonium triggered them aggregation, and the breaking of carbon core caused fluorescence quenching. In order to verify the group changes on the surface of CDs-P(Ph)3, the XPS survey spectrum of oxidized carbon dots (CDs-P(Ph)3+HClO) was also carried out (Fig. S6). By comparing the XPS survey spectra of CDs-P(Ph)3 and oxidized carbon dots, it could be easily founded that the count of O 1s located at 531 eV significant increased, while that of C 1s peak located at 284 eV decreased highly (Fig. S6A), meaning that the skeleton structures of CDs-P(Ph)3 were destroyed by HClO. Especially, the high-resolution O 1s XPS spectrum containing four peaks at 531.2 eV, 532.6 eV, 533.3 eV and 534.5 eV severally originated from O–CO, CO, C–O–C/C–OH and N–O, which implied the formation of new bonds C–OH and N–O on the surface of oxidized carbon dots (Fig. S6D) [36,37]. Additionally, the counts of N 1s and P 2p of carbon dots also obviously decreased in comparison with that of CDs-P(Ph)3 (Fig. 4 A, C, E), demonstrating the destroy of CO–NH bond by HClO. After CDs-P(Ph)3 reacting with HClO, a new peak at 198 eV was attributable to Cl 2p. The high-resolution Cl 2p XPS spectrum consisting of two peaks at 197.5 eV, 198.5 eV and 199.3 eV was assigned to C–Cl, N–Cl and CO–Cl, respectively (Fig. S6F) [38]. The results of XPS spectra verified the diminution of carbon dots size and the cleavage of cationic group from carbon dots’ surface, which had a good agreement with the phenomenon displayed by the TEM.

Next, the photophysical property of CDs-P(Ph)3 in PBS buffer solution (10 mM, pH = 7.4) for HClO was studied by a UV–vis spectra. As shown in Fig. 3C (black curve), the UV–vis spectrum of CDs-P(Ph)3 had three peaks at 209 nm, 290 nm and 381 nm, which could be assigned to the π-π* (CC/CN) and n-π* (CN/C–N/C–O) transition, respectively [39]. With the addition of HClO, the peaks at 209 nm and 381 nm decreased obviously, while the peak at 290 nm greatly increased (red curve). This is because part unsaturated bonds (CC/CN) in the structure of CDs-P(Ph)3 were broken by strong oxidant HClO, leading to the damage of π-conjugated system of carbon dots. Meanwhile, a new absorption peak emerged at the long wavelength of 481 nm, largely resulting from the aggregation of the destroyed CDs-P(Ph)3 in water. In addition, the FT-IR spectra were also performed to analyze the changes of chemical bonds in CDs-P(Ph)3 after reacting with HClO. As shown in Fig. 3D, it could be seen that the peaks around 3200-3500 cm−1 and at1640 cm−1 were attributed to N–H stretching vibration and the asymmetric stretching vibrations of CO, respectively [40]. Different form the spectrum of CDs-P(Ph)3, the peak around 3200-3500 cm−1 was a wide absorption peak attributed to O–H/N–H stretching vibration after reacted with HClO [41], resulting from the destruction of amido bond (CO–NH). Moreover, some new peaks at 1450 cm−1, 940 cm−1 and 770 cm−1 severally attributed to asymmetric stretching vibrations of –NO2, symmetric stretching vibrations of C–O and C–Cl, which largely resulted from the oxidation of –NH2, CC or CN by HClO.

Additionally, different concentrations of HClO (0–80 μM) were severally added into the CDs-P(Ph)3 solution, followed by reacting for 3 min, to obtained their UV–vis spectra (Fig. S7). With the addition of HClO, the peak at 210 nm regularly weakened and exhibited a good linear relation (R2 = 0.9923) between (I0/I) with HClO concertation. The limit of detection of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO was about 0.77 μM, meaning the possibility of CDs-P(Ph)3 for identifying HClO using a spectrophotometric method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The sensitivity and selectivity of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of CDs-P(Ph)3 for the quantitative analysis of HClO were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. As shown in Fig. 4A, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-P(Ph)3 decreased gradually with the increasing HClO concentration. Interestingly, after the reaction between CDs-P(Ph)3 and HClO, the fluorescence emission peak showed a significant blue shift, which was consistent with the results of Fig. 1C, and the blue shift trend was related to the concentration of HClO. This mainly because the fluorescent carbon core of CDs-P(Ph)3 was broken into small size particle (Fig. 3B), and its smaller π-conjugate structure emitted a short wavelength fluorescence. Meanwhile, the auxochromic groups –NH2 on the surface of CDs-P(Ph)3 were oxidized to –NO2 groups (Fig. 3D), inducing the significant fluorescence quenching. And their corresponding photographs were taken by a digital camera under a 365 nm portable UV lamp, exhibiting an obvious fluorescence quenching with the increasing HClO concentrations (insets of Fig. 4A). As the emission peak of CDs-P(Ph)3 shifted blue at the high concentration of HClO, there were two linear relationships with R2 = 0.9975 and R2 = 0.9930 in the range of 0–20 μM and 30–70 μM between log (I0/I) and HClO concentrations, respectively (Fig. 4B). According to the equation LOD = 3σ/k, where σ was the standard deviation of blank signal and k was the slope of linear calibration plot [42], the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.32 μM (Fig. 4B). These results indicated that CDs-P(Ph)3 could be used as a good fluorescence sensor for the trace detection of HClO in water.

High selectivity of probe for aim substance is the key factor to evaluate whether it is suitable to serve as a sensor. Hence, the species, namely 80 μM HClO and 100 μM (O2 −, H2O2, •OH, TBHP, 1O2, TBO•, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Mg2+ and Cr2+), were severally added into the solution of CDs-P(Ph)3 to obtained their corresponding fluorescence spectra (see gray column of Fig. 4C, Fig. S8). It was easily founded that the above species only had a slight influence on the fluorescence of CDs-P(Ph)3. However, HClO caused a prominent fluorescence quenching with a high quenching effective about 99% according to the equation η = (I0–I)/I0, showing the high selectivity and sensitivity of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO. In addition, the interference experiment of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO was also performed in the presence of other species (red column of Fig. 4C). The results revealed that the fluorescence of CDs-P(Ph)3 could be also quenched completely by HClO in the presence of other oxide species or metal cations, meaning its remarkable anti-interference performance. In short, the high selective and anti-interference properties of CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO made it suitable to serve as a chemosensor in real sample.

For testing the practicality and accuracy of CDs-P(Ph)3, the experiment of recovery rates for HClO in tap, lake and well water was performed, and their corresponding data were listed in Table 2 . Firstly, we took a certain volume of tap, lake and well water, in which HClO amount was negligible to prepared the detection solution of CDs-P(Ph)3. And different amounts of HClO were added into the above detection solutions to make their final concentration reach to 5, 10 and 15 μM, respectively. After a completive reaction time of 3 min, the found amounts of HClO in water were obtained according to Fig. 4B. It could be seen that CDs-P(Ph)3 could accurately and practically detect HClO in real water samples with a relative standard deviation lower than 4%. Most importantly, the remarkable dispersion stability of CDs-P(Ph)3 in an aqueous solution for long time made it ideal for the on-site and real-time detection of analytes.

Here, a kind of cationic carbon dots CDs-P(Ph)3 has been prepared, severally using m-phenylenediamine and 4-carboxybutyl triphenylphosphonium bromide as the carbon source and cationic source by hydrothermal method. Due to the strong electron-withdrawing effect of triphenylphosphonium, CDs-P(Ph)3 emitting bright green fluorescence not only can disperse stably with the high concentration for a long time, but also exhibits a higher sensitivity for HClO than that of CDs. The limit of detection CDs-P(Ph)3 for HClO is as low as 0.32 μM. In addition, CDs-P(Ph)3 exhibits a good selectivity and anti-interference ability to achieve accurate and rapid detection of HClO. Importantly, CDs-P(Ph)3 has been successfully applied in the detection of HClO in tap, lake and well water with a high accuracy. However, CDs-P(Ph)3 that we prepared in this work was an intensity-based fluorescent sensor, which outputs a relatively single detection signal in comparison with the ratiometric fluorescent sensors. It will be a good idea to construct a kind of cationic ratiometric fluorescent CDs to obtain more obvious signal outputs for analytes detection. Although such easily obtained CDs-P(Ph)3 has the above-mentioned disadvantage, it still can provide a practical application pathway for optical sensing detection due to the solution ultra-stability and its rapid/on-site monitoring for HClO in water samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22274158, 21804100, 21976183), Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Provincial (2208085MB30), Anhui West University Scientific Research Foundation for High-Level Talents (WGKQ2021001, WGKQ202001002 and WGKQ201702002), Nature Science Research Project of Colleges and Universities in Anhui Province (KJ2020ZD57), the Postdoctoral Research Project of West Anhui University (WXBSH2021003, WSBSH2020001) and Anhui Provincial outstanding Young Talents Support Program for Colleges and Universities (gxyqZD2022073).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dynamic mapping of spontaneously produced H2S in the entire cell space and in live animals using a rationally designed molecular switch

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Yang, J. Zhao, X. Yu, R. Zhang, G. Han, R. Liu, Z. Liu, T. Zhao, M.-Y. Han, Z. Zhang, Dynamic mapping of spontaneously produced H2S in the entire cell space and in live animals using a rationally designed molecular switch, Analyst 143 (2018) 1881-1889.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Linlin Yang obtained her Ph.D. degree from University of Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2018. During 2017–2020, she worked as a postdoctoral associate at Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. She is currently working as an associate professor in West Anhui University. Her research interests focus on the design and synthesis of fluorescent probe for the detection of bioactive molecules in living cells and animals.

Lingfei Li is working at Institute of Solid State Physics, Hefei Institutes of Physical Science, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research is focused on the development of nano-chemical and optical sensors.

Renyong Liu obtained his Ph.D. degree from University of Science and Technology of China in 2018. During 2009–2018, he worked as a research assistant at Institute of Intelligent Machines, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He is currently working as an associate professor in West Anhui University. His research interests focus on the design and synthesis of nanoprobes for the detection of toxic and harmful substances in the environment and organisms.

Lijuan Chen obtained her Ph.D. degree from University of Science and Technology of China in 2014. During 2014–2016, she worked as a postdoctoral associate at University of Science and Technology of China. She is currently working as a lecturer in West Anhui University. Her research interests focus on the design and synthesis of polymer fluorescent probe for the detection of metal ions、 bioactive molecules in living cells and animals.

Yehan Yan obtained her Ph.D. degree from USTC (University of Science and Technology of China) in 2017. She is currently working as a lecturer in West Anhui University. Her research interests focus on the design and synthesis of fluorescent probe and its application in the detection of environmental pollutants such as heavy metals, hydrogen sulfide, nitrogen dioxide.

Chenggen Xie obtained his Ph.D. from Anhui University in 2007. He is currently working as a professor at West Anhui University. His research interests focus on electrochemical sensors and biosensors, modified electrodes and the molecular imprinting.

Wengui Chang obtained his Ph.D. from Anhui University in 2009. He is currently working as a professor at West Anhui University. His research interests focus on the synthesis and properties of nanometer photoelectric materials, and electroanalytical chemistry.

Ning-Ning Zhang obtained her Ph.D. degree in Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2019. She is currently a lecturer at Liaocheng University. Her current research fields focus on the development of novel photoelectric functional crystalline materials.

Wei Zhang obtained her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2016. She is currently an associate professor at Liaocheng University. Her current research fields focus on the development of novel electrochemiluminescent material for biosensors.

Jun Zhao obtained her Ph.D. degree from University of Science and Technology of China in 2013. She is currently working as a research assistant at Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her current research interests focus on the application of nanomaterials in detection and diagnosis of markers of cancer in living cells and animals.

Bianhua Liu obtained her Ph.D. degree from Chinese Academy of Sciences in 2012. She is currently working as an associate professor at Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her research interests focus on the design and synthesis of nano-materials for detection of environmental pollutants.

Liang Yang obtained his Ph.D. degree from University of Science and Technology of China (USTC) in 2015. He is currently working as an associate researcher at Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His current research interests focus on the application of nano-chemical and optical sensors in environmental trace hazard detection and biochemical sensing.
